steps.dialogue: You've been working [event:68fbe26c6e54bf85] with unit fractions - [event:68fbe33513af5279]creating them [delay] , [event:68fbe3de327c95e9]identifying them[d] , comparing them. Next, let's see how they connect to bigger mathematical ideas.
Remember how you noticed that one-eighth [event:68fbe58f129e5d4d] is tiny compared to one-half? There's a pattern here.
Look at these fractions from biggest[event:68fbe663cdb4e60a]  to smallest. Think about what happens to the bottom number as the pieces get smaller.
The bottom number gets BIGGER [event:68fbee9b5da6b03e]as the pieces get SMALLER! That's a key idea in math!
[event:68fbe1e83a616fef] The bottom number shows us how many pieces the bar is divided into[event:68fbef095d40c175]. When we split the bar into 8 pieces[event:68fbf039f1bfb73e], each piece is small.

This pattern has a fancy name. We call it an
 [vocab]inverse relationship[/vocab].  [event:68ff896416e91c51] It shows up everywhere in math and in life. Splitting a whole [event:68fbf1e72e329845] into more pieces means each one is smaller.
But when the whole is in fewer equal pieces.[event:68fc03601f6c4d0e] , it means each piece is bigger. Watch when we double the bottom number, [event:68fc014df9ff327d]the denominator.

[event:68fc0202a6056fee] When we go from one-half to one-fourth[event:68ff89d3a08b9c36] , we doubled the bottom number from 2 to 4. And the piece is half [event:68ff8abd3bad5df0]
the size!
Same thing here[event:68fc046dcb2807d0].  When we go from one-third to [event:68fc04c4010219ae]one-sixth - we double from 3 to 6, and again,[event:68fc0563f112dde9] the piece is half the size.
[event:68fc05d880140dbb]When you double the bottom number, the piece [event:68fc05ef548a2207] gets half as big. We'll keep working to remember that pattern.
[vocab]Unit fractions[/vocab] aren't just ideas for math class.

We use these bars to understand the IDEA of 
[vocab]unit fractions[/vocab], but in real life, they come in all shapes.

When you and 7 [event:68fc07ff05749b5d]friends share a pizza equally - everyone gets [event:68fc082ccfb70b9d]one-eighth! When a recipe calls for [event:68fc085dd113b217]one-fourth cup of sugar, it's this same idea. When you break off one piece of a 6-piece[event:68fc0895abf6df0c]  chocolate bar, that's one-sixth![event:68fc08b5c0f858b2]
The SHAPE might be different - pizzas are circles, measuring cups are cylinders - but the MATH is exactly the same. One part [event:68fc0935cca21fc5] out of equal parts[event:68ff8c38ffa4a688].

Let's see if I've explained this idea so it sticks. 
Look at these bars and think about the amounts. Click the unit fraction that gives you the smallest portion.

prompt.text: Click on the bar that shows the smallest portion.

step.dialogue: Not quite. Remember our pattern - more parts means smaller pieces.

[event:68ff8db4a3cefa2f] Look at this size comparison - see how the [fraction numerator=1, denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] piece is much smaller? More friends sharing means smaller portions for each.
[event:real_world_connection_hv] Let me show you the pattern: More [event:694af61cfaeb7dca] divisions means smaller pieces for each person.

on_correct.dialogue: Right. The one-eighth bar shows the smallest portion. When that pizza is divided 8 ways, each person gets the smallest slice [event:68ff8e44d56042fe] . You're using unit fractions to understand real situations!

steps.dialogue: Every time you share something equally, every time you measure in cooking, [event:68ff8f77cc5fd9f4]even when you talk about time like [event:68ff8efcf9e1e3be] 'half hour' - you're using unit fractions.
You've learned a lot about unit fractions and how all fractions work.

[event:68ffa64bb71ea54c] You learned that [vocab]unit fractions[/vocab] always show exactly ONE [event:68ff90b8db3bbfea] part of a whole. The bigger the denominator, the smaller the piece. These patterns show up in everyday life.
Our practice helps us to understand the reasons, not just the rules.
Next time, we'll learn what happens when we want to show MORE [event:68ff91919f941837]than one part. How do we build bigger fractions from unit fractions?
Whether you like math or not, notice that when math clicks it feels good, doesn't it? That's your brain getting stronger at problem solving.
Next lesson, we'll take everything you know about unit fractions and use them as building blocks to create any fraction.
